data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: setForNextEpoch(uint64 _epochIntervalMicros) requires GOVERNANCE; only reverts if _epochIntervalMicros == 0 (line 105-107). No upper bound check. Stores _pendingEpochIntervalMicros = _epochIntervalMicros (line 109) and sets hasPendingConfig = true.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Caller must satisfy requireAllowed(SystemAddresses.GOVERNANCE) and contract must be initialized.

data_flow_trace.original: At next epoch transition, _applyReconfiguration() calls EpochConfig.applyPendingConfig(); epochIntervalMicros becomes type(uint64).max
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _applyReconfiguration() invokes EpochConfig(...).applyPendingConfig() at line 274. In EpochConfig.applyPendingConfig() (src/runtime/EpochConfig.sol:122-138), epochIntervalMicros = _pendingEpochIntervalMicros unconditionally (line 132) whenever hasPendingConfig is true; there is no re-validation or bound check.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Caller must be RECONFIGURATION contract (line 123). hasPendingConfig must be true (line 126).

data_flow_trace.original: lastReconfigurationTime is set to current timestamp (~1.7e15) during applyReconfiguration
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: After incrementing epoch, lastReconfigurationTime = ITimestamp(TIMESTAMP).nowMicroseconds() (line 293). nowMicroseconds returns block-time in microseconds, a nonzero large value (>0) for any running chain.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Execution path reaches line 293 (inside _applyReconfiguration); guaranteed after applyPendingConfig succeeds.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: onBlockStart() (line 85) unconditionally calls IReconfiguration(RECONFIGURATION).checkAndStartTransition() at line 112 on every block. Not wrapped in try/catch: a revert in checkAndStartTransition propagates and reverts the entire block prologue.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: Caller must be SYSTEM_CALLER (line 90) and _initialized must be true (line 91). This is the normal runtime entry point called by VM each block.

data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: checkAndStartTransition() invokes _canTransition() unconditionally at line 94 (after a short-circuit if DkgInProgress). If DKG is not in progress (idle state after completing last epoch), _canTransition() is always called.

data_flow_trace.original: _canTransition() computes lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval; uint64 checked addition overflows, reverts
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: _canTransition() reads uint64 epochInterval from EpochConfig (line 217), then evaluates `lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval` in a uint64 checked arithmetic context (line 218). Solidity ^0.8.30 default semantics: no `unchecked` block surrounds this expression (verified — zero `unchecked` occurrences in Reconfiguration.sol), so overflow reverts with Panic(0x11). When epochInterval == type(uint64).max and lastReconfigurationTime > 0, the sum exceeds uint64 range and reverts.
data_flow_trace.gating_conditions: epochInterval was set to uint64.max and lastReconfigurationTime > 0 (true for any post-genesis chain, since nowMicroseconds() returns the block time in microseconds).

data_flow_trace.original: Revert propagates through checkAndStartTransition() -> onBlockStart() — no try/catch — block processing halts
data_flow_trace.current_state_summary: Line 112 calls IReconfiguration(RECONFIGURATION).checkAndStartTransition() as a plain external call with no try/catch. A revert inside _canTransition (which is used by checkAndStartTransition) bubbles up through onBlockStart, reverting the block prologue and halting forward progress. Note: Blocker emits `ComponentUpdateFailed` events elsewhere (line 41), but that pattern is NOT applied here — the reconfiguration call is uncaught.

reasoning_chain: All 7 steps in the reported data-flow are verifiable against the current code at commit a623eab. (1) EpochConfig.setForNextEpoch (src/runtime/EpochConfig.sol:99-113) validates only `_epochIntervalMicros == 0`; no upper bound. (2) Reconfiguration._applyReconfiguration() propagates the pending value into live state via EpochConfig.applyPendingConfig (src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:274 -> src/runtime/EpochConfig.sol:132), which never re-validates. (3) lastReconfigurationTime is updated to a large nonzero value (src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:293). (4-5) Every block, Blocker.onBlockStart (src/blocker/Blocker.sol:112) calls checkAndStartTransition, which reaches _canTransition (src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:94). (6) _canTransition performs `currentTime >= lastReconfigurationTime + epochInterval` (src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:218) in checked uint64 arithmetic. Solidity 0.8.30 default-checked semantics and absence of any `unchecked` block confirm that `lastReconfigurationTime + type(uint64).max` reverts with Panic(0x11). (7) The revert propagates unhandled through onBlockStart (no try/catch at src/blocker/Blocker.sol:112), halting every subsequent block. Additionally, getRemainingTimeSeconds (src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:189-201) performs the same vulnerable addition at line 194, so view calls also revert. Recovery is possible only via governanceReconfigure (src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:141-166) which bypasses _canTransition; this matches the exploit description exactly and confirms the chain-halt/DoS impact is real and reachable.

key_evidence.why_matters: Shows that only the != 0 check guards the governance-settable interval; type(uint64).max is accepted.
The vulnerable addition. Executed on every block via checkAndStartTransition; checked uint64 overflow reverts block processing.
Secondary occurrence of the same vulnerable addition in a view function; confirms pattern and widens DoS to off-chain tooling / consumers.

key_evidence.excerpt:
// 3. Check and potentially start epoch transition
IReconfiguration(SystemAddresses.RECONFIGURATION).checkAndStartTransition();
key_evidence.why_matters: No try/catch; revert inside _canTransition bubbles out and halts onBlockStart for every block.
Governance recovery path does not call _canTransition, so it remains reachable even after the chain is halted — matching the reported two-tx recovery.

counterargument: One might argue that uint64 overflow is implausible because `lastReconfigurationTime` is small relative to `type(uint64).max` and governance is trusted. However, nowMicroseconds() represents real time in microseconds, which for any post-genesis chain is already ~1.7e15 (Unix epoch × 1e6) — strictly greater than 0 and much larger than 0 — so `lastReconfigurationTime + type(uint64).max` unambiguously overflows uint64 (max ≈ 1.84e19). Even if `lastReconfigurationTime` were just 1, `1 + type(uint64).max` still overflows. The only requirement is `lastReconfigurationTime > 0`, which is guaranteed post-initialization (src/blocker/Reconfiguration.sol:70). The trust argument fails because (a) governance is multi-sig/DAO-controlled and a single mis-parameterization (e.g. passing microseconds as nanoseconds, or using uint64.max as a sentinel) is realistic, and (b) the only input validation on a safety-critical parameter is `!= 0`. A defensible design would cap to, e.g., 31 days in microseconds, mirroring bounds used elsewhere (cf. ExcessiveLockupDuration / VotingDurationTooLong errors in Errors.sol). The absence of such a cap is a verifiable bug, not a theoretical one.
related_existing_tests: test/unit/runtime/EpochConfig.t.sol — test suite for setForNextEpoch covers zero-reject and basic happy path but contains no test for uint64.max or any upper-bound rejection.
test/unit/blocker/Reconfiguration.t.sol — covers _canTransition timing logic but does not fuzz or boundary-test epochInterval near uint64.max.
